Were You Asked This?
Design an Image Uploader

Design a system for collecting Street View images from taxi-mounted cameras that can handle high-volume uploads, deduplication, and prepare images for privacy/quality processing.

Asked at:

Google

A Street View Image Uploader is a service that ingests 360° images captured by taxi-mounted cameras, reliably transfers them over cellular networks, deduplicates overlapping shots, and prepares durable references and metadata for downstream privacy/quality processing—similar in spirit to how Google Street View or Mapillary collect roadway imagery at scale. Drivers just drive; the on-vehicle hardware captures images, buffers them, and uploads them when connectivity allows.

Interviewers ask this to probe how you design edge-to-cloud ingestion of large blobs at high write rates, handle unreliable networks, ensure idempotency and deduplication, and orchestrate multi-step pipelines. They’re also testing your ability to manage backpressure, cost, geospatial metadata, and operational concerns like monitoring, rollout, and data lifecycle.

Community Solutions
Design an Image Uploader
by Jujabi
0
1
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to capture and upload 360° street images with GPS, timestamp, and heading metadata from taxi-mounted cameras.

Users should be able to reliably transfer large image files over unreliable cellular networks with resumability and idempotent retries.

Users should be able to have duplicate and near-duplicate images automatically detected and collapsed into a canonical record.

Users should be able to have accepted images durably queued and discoverable by downstream privacy/quality processing.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you achieve reliable, resumable uploads from moving taxis with intermittent connectivity and variable bandwidth?

How will you do scalable deduplication (exact and near-duplicate) without turning the upload path into a bottleneck?

How do you implement backpressure and adaptive capture rates so you don’t overwhelm networks, storage, or downstream processors?

How will you structure metadata, lineage, and handoff to privacy/quality pipelines to ensure durability, ordering, and traceability?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

Images are large binary objects. Efficient ingestion requires chunked uploads, pre-signed URLs, and storing blobs in object storage while keeping metadata in a separate store. This pattern prevents hot application servers and databases from becoming throughput bottlenecks.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Thousands of taxis can generate sustained high write rates. You need techniques like horizontal sharding, append-only logs for ingestion, idempotent write paths, and backpressure to safely scale the write-heavy pipeline.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

The flow spans capture, upload, dedup, and handoff to downstream privacy/quality processing. Modeling this as a multi-step workflow with durable state and retries ensures correctness, observability, and recoverability across stages.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, high-throughput event log for ingestion events and workflow signaling (e.g., image-ingested, dedup-complete). It enables backpressure, replay, and decouples upload from downstream privacy/quality processors.

Read More
Cassandra

A distributed, write-optimized key-value/column store is ideal for high-velocity metadata (upload sessions, hash indexes, device state) with predictable low-latency writes and horizontal scalability across regions.

Read More
API Gateway

An API Gateway fronts the upload control APIs (create session, list chunks, complete upload) and issues pre-signed URLs for direct-to-object-store transfers, offloading large blob traffic from application servers while centralizing auth and throttling.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Dropbox

Both systems ingest large files from many clients over unreliable networks, require resumable uploads and idempotency, and separate blob storage from metadata with a background deduplication step.

Read
Practice
Instagram

High-volume media ingestion with asynchronous processing pipelines (e.g., quality checks, transformations), metadata indexing, and fanout to downstream services parallels the Street View ingestion pipeline.

Read
Practice
Web Crawler

At-scale content collection, deduplication of near-duplicates, backpressure, and scheduling/control loops to avoid overloading targets map closely to adapting capture rates and managing coverage in a fleet.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Performing expensive deduplication synchronously in the upload request path

This couples user-facing latency to compute-heavy similarity checks, causing timeouts and poor throughput. Dedup should be split: exact hash checks fast, near-duplicate detection asynchronously.

Storing image blobs directly in a relational/metadata database

Mixing large blobs with hot metadata leads to bloated storage, poor cache locality, and slow backups. Use object storage for blobs and a separate scalable metadata store for indexes and workflow state.

Ignoring idempotency and resumability for uploads and events

Without upload session IDs, chunk hashes, and idempotent completion, intermittent networks will create duplicates, corrupt objects, and inconsistent downstream processing. This undermines data quality and inflates costs.

Mid January, 2026

Google

Manager

Late June, 2025

Google

Manager

Google wants to collect Street View images from taxis. You are responsible for designing the system that uploads images from the taxis and stores those images in the cloud for future processing. You can specify the hardware to be deployed in each taxi, and can control when the camera should capture images. The default camera takes 360 degree images. Images on the server will be processed by another layer to manage privacy, quality and consistency. That layer is responsible for storing the fi nal images for Street View users.

Late September, 2024

Google

Manager
